ground_truth.title: Adapt This Japanese Productivity Model to Get More Done
ground_truth.author: Lindsey Ellefson
ground_truth.main_content: So many great productivity hacks come from the Japanese manufacturing industry. The 5S technique helps you keep your workspace clean so you can get more done, kaizen helps you improve the processes and workflow of your job, and kanban helps you schedule your tasks in the most efficient way. These are helpful even if you're not working within the famed Toyota Production System; they're adaptable to fit all kinds of work scenarios, which is similarly true for the 3 Ms that originated there, too. The 3 Ms are kinds of waste you should identify and eliminate to keep your work running smoothly. Lean process thinking, or lean manufacturing, is a production method that seeks to save time within the production process. They are 'muda,' 'mura,' and 'muri,' and they all refer to kinds of wasteful practices that slow you down and keep you from being productive. Muda translates from Japanese to mean 'futility' or 'uselessness.' It's anything that doesn't add value to the work you're doing and it comes in two forms. Mura is any kind of unevenness in your operation. Anything that isn't uniform, regular, or scheduled is mura. Muri is any overburden on a tool or person. It can result from mura, as in the example above, but can also spring from overutilization. To eliminate muda, identify which unnecessary steps you're taking in your work. To eliminate mura, start tracking the process of your work and identify what's holding you up. Finally, to eliminate muri, assess your own burden at work. If you're missing deadlines, feeling overwhelmed, not being given the right amount of resources, or having too much piled on you, you need to streamline what you're working on so you don't completely burn out. Training yourself to recognize and eliminate these different kinds of waste will streamline your work, reduce your burden, and keep things running more smoothly so you can actually be productive.

They are 'muda,' 'mura,' and 'muri,' and they all refer to kinds of wasteful practices that slow you down and keep you from being productive.
Muda translates from Japanese to mean 'futility' or 'uselessness.' It's anything that doesn't add value to the work you're doing.
Muri is any overburden on a tool or person. It can result from mura, as in the example above, but can also spring from overutilization.
Training yourself to recognize and eliminate these different kinds of waste will streamline your work, reduce your burden, and keep things running more smoothly.
without: Lifehacker has been a go-to source
